5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.

To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. 100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.

16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

75	## LSP MCP tools

77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.

79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean

127	## End-of-session handoff

63	/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
64	second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the
65	`X`-axis onto `x₀`" endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y`:
66	`snd ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`.
67	
68	Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
69	`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
70	uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
71	theorem rigidity_snd_lift
72	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
73	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
74	    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
75	      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
76	  ext1 <;> simp
77	
78	/-- **Bridge 1 of the Rigidity Lemma (closed-map step), PROVEN.** When `X` is complete (proper)
79	over `k̄`, the second monoidal projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` has, on underlying schemes, a
80	*closed* base map. This is Mumford's "completeness of `X` makes `p₂` a closed map" (Abelian
81	Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
82	
83	Proof: the underlying scheme morphism `(snd X Y).left` is the pullback projection
84	`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` (`Over.snd_left`), i.e. the base change of `X.hom` along
85	`Y.hom`. `IsProper X.hom ⟹ UniversallyClosed X.hom` (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
86	`UniversallyClosed` is stable under base change
87	(`universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback` on the canonical pullback square), so
88	`(snd X Y).left` is universally closed and hence its base map is closed
89	(`Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap`). Char-free; no theorem of the cube, no cohomology. -/
90	theorem snd_left_isClosedMap
91	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper X.hom] :
92	    IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := by
93	  haveI hp : UniversallyClosed X.hom := IsProper.toUniversallyClosed
94	  haveI : UniversallyClosed (snd X Y).left := by
95	    rw [Over.snd_left]
96	    exact universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback
97	      (IsPullback.of_hasPullback X.hom Y.hom) hp
98	  exact Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap _
99	
100	/-- **Dense-closed-points hom-extensionality (the bespoke globalisation connective, PROVEN).**
101	Two morphisms `g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z` out of a *reduced* scheme `W` whose closed points are *dense*
102	(`[JacobsonSpace W]` — e.g. when `W` is locally of finite type over a field) into a *separated*
103	scheme `Z` are equal as soon as they agree at every closed point `x ∈ closedPoints W` after the
104	canonical residue-field probe `W.fromSpecResidueField x : Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`.
105	
106	This is the one connective the iter-159 `mathlib-analogist` flagged that Mathlib does **not**
107	package directly: Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism `ext_of_isDominant`. Here we
108	assemble all the closed points into one dominant probe — the coproduct
109	`∐_{x ∈ closedPoints W} Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`, whose topological range is exactly the (dense) set of
110	closed points — and feed it to `ext_of_isDominant`. It is `Step 2` of bridge 2's route B
111	(cohomology-free) and is fully proven here, reusable independently of the rigidity context. -/
112	theorem morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints
113	    {W Z : Scheme.{u}} [IsReduced W] [JacobsonSpace W] [Z.IsSeparated]
114	    {g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z}
115	    (h : ∀ x ∈ closedPoints W,
116	      W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₁ = W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₂) :
117	    g₁ = g₂ := by
118	  -- The dominant probe: the coproduct of the residue-field `Spec`s over the closed points.
119	  let F : closedPoints W → Scheme.{u} := fun x => Spec (W.residueField x.1)
120	  let probe : (∐ F) ⟶ W := Sigma.desc fun x => W.fromSpecResidueField x.1
121	  -- Its topological range contains every closed point, hence (Jacobson) is dense.
122	  haveI : IsDominant probe := by
123	    refine ⟨(dense_iff_closure_eq.mpr (closure_closedPoints (X := W))).mono ?_⟩
124	    intro x hx

137	/-- **A proper integral `k̄`-scheme mapping into an affine is constant on `k̄`-points (the deep
138	algebraic content of Step 1, PROVEN).** Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, let `W` be an
139	integral scheme that is universally closed and locally of finite type over `Spec k̄` (e.g. a proper
140	integral slice `X_y`), and let `g : W ⟶ V` be a morphism into an *affine* scheme `V`. Then `g`
141	takes the same value on any two `k̄`-points (sections `a`, `b` of the structure map `wk`):
142	`a ≫ wk = 𝟙` and `b ≫ wk = 𝟙` force `a ≫ g = b ≫ g`.
143	
144	This is the cohomology-free realisation of "a global regular function on a proper integral
145	`k̄`-variety is constant". The global sections `Γ(W, ⊤)` form a field
146	(`isField_of_universallyClosed`) that is module-finite over `k̄`
147	(`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`, hence the structure map is integral on `Γ`); algebraic
148	closedness collapses the finite extension (`IsAlgClosed.ringHom_bijective_of_isIntegral`), so the
149	structure map's global-sections map `wk.appTop` is an isomorphism. Both sections `a`, `b` are
150	left inverses of `wk.appTop` on `Γ`, hence have equal `appTop`; and a morphism into the affine `V`
151	is pinned by its `appTop` (`ext_of_isAffine`). No coherent cohomology, no relative Stein
152	factorisation. -/
153	theorem eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral
154	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {W : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral W] (wk : W ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar))
156	    [UniversallyClosed wk] [LocallyOfFiniteType wk]
157	    {V : Scheme.{u}} [IsAffine V] (g : W ⟶ V)
158	    (a b : Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) ⟶ W)
159	    (ha : a ≫ wk = 𝟙 _) (hb : b ≫ wk = 𝟙 _) :
160	    a ≫ g = b ≫ g := by
161	  -- `Γ(W)` is a field (proper integral over `k̄`).
162	  letI : Field Γ(W, ⊤) := (isField_of_universallyClosed (CommRingCat.of kbar) wk).toField
163	  -- `F : k̄ ⟶ Γ(W)` (the structure ring map up to `ΓSpecIso`) is integral, hence — `k̄` alg-closed,
164	  -- `Γ(W)` a domain — bijective, so an iso.
165	  set F : CommRingCat.of kbar ⟶ Γ(W, ⊤) :=
166	    (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv ≫ wk.appTop with hF
167	  have hint : F.hom.IsIntegral := by
168	    apply RingHom.isIntegral_respectsIso.2 (e := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso _).symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv)
169	    exact isIntegral_appTop_of_universallyClosed wk
170	  haveI : IsIso F := (ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective F).mpr
171	    (IsAlgClosed.ringHom_bijective_of_isIntegral F.hom hint)
172	  -- Hence `wk.appTop = ΓSpecIso.hom ≫ F` is an iso.
173	  haveI : IsIso wk.appTop := by
174	    have heq : wk.appTop = (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).hom ≫ F := by
175	      rw [hF]; simp
176	    rw [heq]; infer_instance
177	  -- Both sections invert `wk.appTop` on global sections, so they have equal `appTop`.
178	  have haa : wk.appTop ≫ a.appTop = 𝟙 _ := by rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, ha]; simp
179	  have hbb : wk.appTop ≫ b.appTop = 𝟙 _ := by rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, hb]; simp
180	  have hab : a.appTop = b.appTop := by rw [← cancel_epi wk.appTop, haa, hbb]
181	  -- A map into the affine `V` is pinned by `appTop`.
182	  apply ext_of_isAffine
183	  rw [Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, hab]

185	/-- **Integrality descends to a retract (blueprint `lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral`).**
186	If `T` is an integral scheme and `S` is a *retract* of `T` — i.e. there are `r : S ⟶ T` and
187	`pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` — then `S` is integral.
188	
189	Two halves, both elementary (no cohomology):
190	* *Irreducible.* `pr` is a continuous surjection (it has the section `r`, so `pr.base ∘ r.base =
191	  id`), and the continuous surjective image of the irreducible `T` is irreducible.
192	* *Reduced.* For each `x : S`, the stalk map `pr.stalkMap (r x) : 𝒪_{S,x} ⟶ 𝒪_{T, r x}` is split
193	  injective: composing with `r.stalkMap x` gives `(r ≫ pr).stalkMap x`, an isomorphism (since
194	  `r ≫ pr = 𝟙`). Hence `𝒪_{S,x}` embeds into the reduced stalk `𝒪_{T, r x}`
195	  (`isReduced_of_injective`), so every stalk of `S` is reduced (`isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`).
196	
197	Reduced and irreducible together give `IsIntegral S`
198	(`isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced`). This feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly, where
199	the proper slice `X_y ≅ X` must be presented as proper *integral*. -/
200	theorem isIntegral_of_retract {S T : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral T]
201	    (r : S ⟶ T) (pr : T ⟶ S) (hrp : r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S) : IsIntegral S := by
202	  -- `pr.base` is surjective: `r.base` is a section of it.
203	  have hsurj : Function.Surjective pr.base := by
204	    intro x
205	    refine ⟨r.base x, ?_⟩
206	    have h := congrArg (fun m => m.base x) hrp
207	    simpa using h
208	  -- Irreducibility: continuous surjective image of the irreducible `T`.
209	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace S := by
210	    rw [irreducibleSpace_def]
211	    have h := (IrreducibleSpace.isIrreducible_univ T).image pr.base
212	      pr.base.hom.continuous.continuousOn
213	    rwa [Set.image_univ, hsurj.range_eq] at h
214	  -- Reducedness: each stalk of `S` embeds into the corresponding reduced stalk of `T`.
215	  haveI hstalk : ∀ x : S, _root_.IsReduced (S.presheaf.stalk x) := by
216	    intro x
217	    -- `(r ≫ pr).stalkMap x` is an isomorphism (`r ≫ pr = 𝟙`), equal to
218	    -- `pr.stalkMap (r x) ≫ r.stalkMap x`, so the first factor is injective.
219	    haveI hiso : IsIso ((r ≫ pr).stalkMap x) := by
220	      rw [Scheme.Hom.stalkMap_congr_hom (r ≫ pr) (𝟙 S) hrp x, Scheme.Hom.stalkMap_id]
221	      exact inferInstanceAs (IsIso ((S.presheaf.stalkCongr _).hom ≫ 𝟙 _))
222	    rw [Scheme.Hom.stalkMap_comp] at hiso
223	    have hbij := (ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective
224	      (pr.stalkMap (r.base x) ≫ r.stalkMap x)).1 hiso
225	    have hinj : Function.Injective (pr.stalkMap (r.base x)).hom := by
226	      intro a b hab
227	      apply hbij.injective
228	      rw [ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ConcreteCategory.comp_apply]
229	      exact congrArg (r.stalkMap x) hab
230	    have hb : pr.base (r.base x) = x := by
231	      have h := congrArg (fun m => m.base x) hrp
232	      simpa using h
233	    have hred : _root_.IsReduced (S.presheaf.stalk (pr.base (r.base x))) :=
234	      isReduced_of_injective (pr.stalkMap (r.base x)).hom hinj
235	    rwa [hb] at hred
236	  haveI : IsReduced S := isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk S
237	  exact isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced S

17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and

20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube, through a short
36	additive-structure chain: additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
37	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5); the fact that a pointed regular
38	map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne
39	Corollary~1.2); the everywhere-definedness of a rational map from a nonsingular variety into
40	an abelian variety (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}, Milne Theorem~3.2); and the
41	$\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility that forces a pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ to be
42	constant (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, the core of Milne Proposition~3.9). Combining these
43	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
44	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
45	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
46	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
47	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
48	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
49	fully decoupled from Route~A.
50	
51	\medskip
52	
53	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
54	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
55	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
56	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
57	
58	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
59	
60	\begin{lemma}
61	
62	\leanok
63	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
64	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
66	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
68	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
69	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a

72	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
73	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
74	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
75	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
76	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
77	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
78	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
79	\end{lemma}

89	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our

122	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
123	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
124	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
125	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
126	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
127	
128	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
129	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
130	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
131	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
132	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
133	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
134	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
135	
136	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
137	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
138	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
139	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
140	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
141	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
142	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
143	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
144	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
145	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

148	\begin{remark}
149	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
150	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
151	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
152	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
153	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the

215	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
216	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
217	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the

220	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
221	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
222	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
223	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
224	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
225	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
226	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]

228	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
229	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
230	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
231	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
232	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.

234	  \medskip

236	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
237	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
238	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
239	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
240	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
241	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
242	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
243	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.

/-!
# Yoneda embedding of `Grp C`

We show that group objects are exactly those whose yoneda presheaf is a presheaf of groups,
by constructing the yoneda embedding `Grp C ⥤ Cᵒᵖ ⥤ GrpCat.{v}` and
showing that it is fully faithful and its (essential) image is the representable functors.
-/

@[expose] public section

assert_not_exists Field

open CategoryTheory MonoidalCategory Limits Opposite CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj

namespace CategoryTheory
universe w v u
variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] [CartesianMonoidalCategory C]
  {M G H X Y : C} [MonObj M] [GrpObj G] [GrpObj H]

variable (X) in
/-- If `X` represents a presheaf of monoids, then `X` is a monoid object. -/
@[to_additive (attr := implicit_reducible)
/-- If `X` represents a presheaf of additive monoids, then `X` is an additive monoid object. -/]
def GrpObj.ofRepresentableBy (F : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ GrpCat.{w}) (α : (F ⋙ forget _).RepresentableBy X) :
    GrpObj X where
  __ := MonObj.ofRepresentableBy X (F ⋙ forget₂ GrpCat MonCat) α
  inv := α.homEquiv'.symm (α.homEquiv (𝟙 _))⁻¹
  left_inv := by
    change lift (α.homEquiv'.symm (α.homEquiv (𝟙 X))⁻¹) (𝟙 X) ≫
      α.homEquiv'.symm (α.homEquiv' (fst X X) * α.homEquiv' (snd X X)) =
        toUnit X ≫ α.homEquiv'.symm 1
    apply α.homEquiv'.injective
    simp only [α.homEquiv'_comp, Equiv.apply_symm_apply, map_mul, map_one]
    simp only [← α.homEquiv'_comp, Functor.comp_obj, lift_fst, Equiv.apply_symm_apply, lift_snd]
    exact inv_mul_cancel (α.homEquiv (𝟙 X))
  right_inv := by
    change lift (𝟙 X) (α.homEquiv'.symm (α.homEquiv' (𝟙 X))⁻¹) ≫
      α.homEquiv'.symm (α.homEquiv' (fst X X) * α.homEquiv' (snd X X)) =
        toUnit X ≫ α.homEquiv'.symm 1
    apply α.homEquiv'.injective
    simp only [α.homEquiv'_comp, Equiv.apply_symm_apply, map_mul, map_one]
    simp only [← α.homEquiv'_comp]
    simp

/-- If `G` is a group object, then `Hom(X, G)` has a group structure. -/
@[to_additive
/-- If `G` is an additive group object, then `Hom(X, G)` has an additive group structure. -/]
abbrev Hom.group : Group (X ⟶ G) where
  inv f := f ≫ ι
  inv_mul_cancel f := calc
    lift (f ≫ ι) f ≫ μ
    _ = (f ≫ lift ι (𝟙 G)) ≫ μ := by simp
    _ = toUnit X ≫ η := by rw [Category.assoc]; simp

scoped[CategoryTheory.MonObj] attribute [instance] Hom.group
scoped[CategoryTheory.AddMonObj] attribute [instance] Hom.addGroup

@[to_additive]
lemma Hom.inv_def (f : X ⟶ G) : f⁻¹ = f ≫ ι := rfl

variable (G) in
/-- If `G` is a group object, then `Hom(-, G)` is a presheaf of groups. -/
@[to_additive (attr := simps)
/-- If `G` is an additive group object, then `Hom(-, G)` is a presheaf of additive groups. -/]
def yonedaGrpObj : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ GrpCat.{v} where
  obj X := GrpCat.of (unop X ⟶ G)
  map φ := GrpCat.ofHom ((yonedaMonObj G).map φ).hom

variable (G) in
/-- If `G` is a monoid object, then `Hom(-, G)` as a presheaf of monoids is represented by `G`. -/
@[to_additive
/-- If `G` is an additive monoid object, then `Hom(-, G)` as a presheaf of additive monoids
is represented by `G`. -/]
def yonedaGrpObjRepresentableBy : (yonedaGrpObj G ⋙ forget _).RepresentableBy G :=
  Functor.representableByEquiv.symm (.refl _)

variable (G) in
@[to_additive]
lemma GrpObj.ofRepresentableBy_yonedaGrpObjRepresentableBy :
    ofRepresentableBy G _ (yonedaGrpObjRepresentableBy G) = ‹GrpObj G› := by
  ext; change lift (fst G G) (snd G G) ≫ μ = μ; rw [lift_fst_snd, Category.id_comp]

variable (X) in
/-- If `X` represents a presheaf of groups `F`, then `Hom(-, X)` is isomorphic to `F` as
a presheaf of groups. -/
@[to_additive (attr := simps! hom inv)
/-- If `X` represents a presheaf of additive groups `F`, then `Hom(-, X)` is isomorphic to `F` as
a presheaf of additive groups. -/]
def yonedaGrpObjIsoOfRepresentableBy (F : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ GrpCat.{v}) (α : (F ⋙ forget _).RepresentableBy X) :
    letI := GrpObj.ofRepresentableBy X F α
    yonedaGrpObj X ≅ F :=
  letI := GrpObj.ofRepresentableBy X F α
  NatIso.ofComponents (fun Y ↦ MulEquiv.toGrpIso
    { toEquiv := α.homEquiv
      map_mul' :=
  ((yonedaMonObjIsoOfRepresentableBy X (F ⋙ forget₂ GrpCat MonCat) α).hom.app Y).hom.map_mul })
      fun φ ↦ GrpCat.hom_ext <| MonoidHom.ext <| α.homEquiv_comp φ.unop

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- The yoneda embedding of `Grp C` into presheaves of groups. -/
@[to_additive (attr := simps)
/-- The yoneda embedding of `AddGrp_C` into presheaves of additive groups. -/]
def yonedaGrp : Grp C ⥤ Cᵒᵖ ⥤ GrpCat.{v} where
  obj G := yonedaGrpObj G.X
  map {G H} ψ := { app Y := GrpCat.ofHom ((yonedaMon.map ψ.hom).app Y).hom }

@[to_additive (attr := reassoc)]
lemma yonedaGrp_naturality (α : yonedaGrpObj G ⟶ yonedaGrpObj H) (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ G) :
    α.app _ (f ≫ g) = f ≫ α.app _ g := congr($(α.naturality f.op) g)

  zero_hom (f) : ζ ≫ f = ζ := by cat_disch
  add_hom (f) : σ ≫ f = (f ⊗ₘ f) ≫ σ := by cat_disch

/-- The property that a morphism between monoid objects is a monoid morphism. -/
@[to_additive]
class IsMonHom (f : M ⟶ N) : Prop where
  one_hom (f) : η ≫ f = η := by cat_disch
  mul_hom (f) : μ ≫ f = (f ⊗ₘ f) ≫ μ := by cat_disch

attribute [to_additive existing (attr := reassoc (attr := simp))] IsMonHom.one_hom IsMonHom.mul_hom

instance : IsMonHom (𝟙 M) where

instance instIsAddMonHomComp {M N O : C} [AddMonObj M] [AddMonObj N] [AddMonObj O]

    [IsAddMonHom f] [IsAddMonHom g] : IsAddMonHom (f ≫ g) where

@[to_additive]
theorem lift_lift_assoc {A : C} {B : C} [MonObj B] (f g h : A ⟶ B) :
    lift (lift f g ≫ μ) h ≫ μ = lift f (lift g h ≫ μ) ≫ μ := by
  have := lift (lift f g) h ≫= mul_assoc B
  rwa [lift_whiskerRight_assoc, lift_lift_associator_hom_assoc, lift_whiskerLeft_assoc] at this

    lift (f ≫ η) g ≫ μ = g := by
  have := lift f g ≫= one_mul B
  rwa [lift_whiskerRight_assoc, lift_leftUnitor_hom] at this

  rwa [lift_whiskerLeft_assoc, lift_rightUnitor_hom] at this

/-! ## The Milne §I.3 chain: additivity and homomorphisms

From the proven `rigidity_lemma` we derive the two additive-structure corollaries of Milne §I.1
that feed the genus-`0` base case (Route C): the additive decomposition of a morphism out of a
product (Corollary 1.5) and the fact that a pointed regular map of abelian varieties is a group
homomorphism (Corollary 1.2). Both are cube-free and cohomology-free — pure consequences of the
Rigidity Lemma and the `GrpObj`-induced group structure on hom-sets
(`CategoryTheory.MonObj.Hom.group`/`Hom.monoid`: `u * v = lift u v ≫ μ`, `u⁻¹ = u ≫ ι`,
`(1 : X ⟶ A) = toUnit X ≫ η`). -/

/-- **Additive decomposition over a product (Milne Corollary 1.5).** Let `V` be complete (proper)
and `V ⊗ W` a variety (geometrically irreducible, reduced, locally of finite type) over an
algebraically closed `k̄`, and let `A` be an abelian variety. Then any morphism `h : V ⊗ W ⟶ A`
based at the identity (`h(v₀, w₀) = η[A]`, encoded `lift v₀ w₀ ≫ h = η[A]`) decomposes — in the
`GrpObj`-induced group on `Hom(V ⊗ W, A)` — as the product of its two axis-restrictions pulled back
along the projections:

  `h = (p ≫ f) · (q ≫ g)`,

where `p = fst V W`, `q = snd V W`, `f = (v ↦ (v, w₀)) ≫ h` is `h|_{V × {w₀}}`, and
`g = (w ↦ (v₀, w)) ≫ h` is `h|_{{v₀} × W}`. The operation `·` is the hom-group multiplication
(`u * v = lift u v ≫ μ`); the statement does **not** assume `A` commutative.

PROOF (Milne, Cor 1.5, §I.1). Form the group difference `φ := h / ((p ≫ f) · (q ≫ g))`. A direct
hom-group computation shows `φ` collapses the complete `V`-axis `V × {w₀}` to the identity (this is
exactly the `_hf` collapse hypothesis of `rigidity_lemma`, using `h(v₀, w₀) = η[A]`), so by the
Rigidity Lemma `φ = q ≫ g'` factors through the second projection; `φ` also vanishes on the
section `{v₀} × W` of `q`, forcing `g' = 1`, hence `φ = 1` and `h = (p ≫ f) · (q ≫ g)`. -/
theorem hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity
    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {V W : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [IsProper V.hom]
    [GeometricallyIrreducible (V ⊗ W).hom]
    [LocallyOfFiniteType (V ⊗ W).hom]
    [IsReduced (V ⊗ W).left]
    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
    (v₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ V)
    (w₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ W)
    (h : V ⊗ W ⟶ A)
    (hh : lift v₀ w₀ ≫ h = η[A]) :
    h = (fst V W ≫ (lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) ≫ h)) *
        (snd V W ≫ (lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ h)) := by
  haveI : IsSeparated A.hom := inferInstance
  -- Name the two axis-restrictions (folding the goal's RHS).
  set f : V ⟶ A := lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) ≫ h with hf
  set g : W ⟶ A := lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ h with hg
  -- Projection/section identities for the two slice sections.
  have hsVfst : lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) ≫ fst V W = 𝟙 V := by simp
  have hsVsnd : lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) ≫ snd V W = toUnit V ≫ w₀ := by simp
  have hsWfst : lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ fst V W = toUnit W ≫ v₀ := by simp
  have hsWsnd : lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ snd V W = 𝟙 W := by simp
  -- The cross-restrictions of `h` are the identity, because `h(v₀, w₀) = η[A]`.
  have hwsW : w₀ ≫ lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) = lift v₀ w₀ := by
    rw [comp_lift, Category.comp_id, ← Category.assoc,
      toUnit_unique (w₀ ≫ toUnit W) (𝟙 _), Category.id_comp]
  have hvsV : v₀ ≫ lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) = lift v₀ w₀ := by
    rw [comp_lift, Category.comp_id, ← Category.assoc,
      toUnit_unique (v₀ ≫ toUnit V) (𝟙 _), Category.id_comp]
  have hwg : w₀ ≫ g = η[A] := by rw [hg, ← Category.assoc, hwsW, hh]
  have hvf : v₀ ≫ f = η[A] := by rw [hf, ← Category.assoc, hvsV, hh]
  -- The group difference `φ = h / (f∘p · g∘q)`.
  set φ : V ⊗ W ⟶ A := h / ((fst V W ≫ f) * (snd V W ≫ g)) with hφ
  -- `φ` collapses the complete `V`-axis `V × {w₀}` to the identity.
  have hcolV : lift (𝟙 V) (toUnit V ≫ w₀) ≫ φ = toUnit V ≫ η[A] := by
    rw [← Hom.one_def, hφ, GrpObj.comp_div, ← hf, MonObj.comp_mul,
      ← Category.assoc, hsVfst, Category.id_comp,
      ← Category.assoc, hsVsnd, Category.assoc, hwg, ← Hom.one_def, mul_one, div_self']
  -- `φ` vanishes on the `{v₀} × W` section of the second projection.
  have hcolW : lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ φ = (1 : W ⟶ A) := by
    rw [hφ, GrpObj.comp_div, ← hg, MonObj.comp_mul,
      ← Category.assoc, hsWfst, Category.assoc, hvf, ← Hom.one_def,
      ← Category.assoc, hsWsnd, Category.id_comp, one_mul, div_self']
  -- Rigidity: `φ` factors through the second projection.
  obtain ⟨g', hg'⟩ := rigidity_lemma φ v₀ w₀ η[A] hcolV
  -- The factor is the identity, read off the `{v₀} × W` section.
  have hg'1 : g' = 1 := by
    have hsec : lift (toUnit W ≫ v₀) (𝟙 W) ≫ φ = g' := by
      rw [hg', ← Category.assoc, hsWsnd, Category.id_comp]
    rw [← hsec, hcolW]
  have hφ1 : φ = 1 := by rw [hg', hg'1, MonObj.comp_one]
  -- Conclude `h = f∘p · g∘q`.
  have hdiv : h / ((fst V W ≫ f) * (snd V W ≫ g)) = 1 := by rw [← hφ]; exact hφ1
  exact div_eq_one.mp hdiv

    [IsReduced (A ⊗ A).left]
    [GrpObj B] [IsProper B.hom] [Smooth B.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible B.hom]

    IsMonHom α := by

  it collapses the `V`-axis to `1` (= `toUnit V ≫ η[A]`, the exact `_hf` of `rigidity_lemma`)
  and vanishes on the `{v₀}×W` section of `q`; `rigidity_lemma` factors `φ = q ≫ g'`, the
  section forces `g' = 1`, so `φ = 1`, and `div_eq_one.mp` gives the decomposition.
- **Key lemmas:** `GrpObj.comp_div`, `MonObj.comp_mul`, `MonObj.comp_one`, `Hom.one_def`,
  `Hom.mul_def`, `comp_lift`, `toUnit_unique`, `div_self'`, `div_eq_one`.
- **Signature:** mirrors `rigidity_lemma`'s instance bundle on `V`/`V⊗W` plus the
  abelian-variety bundle on `A`; conclusion is the explicit-witness equation

- **Key lemmas:** `lift_comp_one_right`, `lift_comp_one_left`, `Hom.mul_def`,
